audit_conclusion: Teacher distillation improves one split at a time and can depress synthetic fidelity; it is not a mechanism-level fix for gauge identifiability.
Domain-weighted distillation uses domain-specific weighting and therefore weakens the clean mechanism claim while still failing to dominate all splits.
Structural backbone changes capacity and improves local behavior, but the gains are architecture-specific rather than an identifiable exposure-field principle.
Increasing synthetic reconstruction protection raises synthetic PSNR but trades away UEFB and real performance, exposing a reconstruction-weight trade-off.
Gate and identity protection can recover isolated split scores, yet the scalar gate remains a fragile controller rather than a calibrated recoverability mechanism.
Domain adapters and affine gates are domain-fragile: improving one domain can damage another and does not produce a stable joint model.
Small real-anchor weights give local real/synthetic changes but do not dominate P3c or justify stronger real-anchor claims.
Larger real anchors remain unstable and are not a safe continuation path under the claim boundary.
DGB routing showed split-wise gains but no row was promoted; the branch is retained as stopped evidence against router revival.
